name: Cordyceps
short_description: A collection of everyday objects—mirror, lamp, and radio—turned into intelligent AI agents that work together as a networked, coordinated autonomous system.
long_description: Cordyceps is a design project by Ethan Chang in which everyday objects such as a mirror, lamp, and radio operate as coordinated agents, with behavior shaped by their individual modalities, physical affordances, functionality, and relations to one another. Rather than relying on fixed smart-home automations or a single humanoid assistant, the system lets a distributed ecology of objects collaborate to perform tasks and interact with users in a more natural, situated way. It matters as physical AI because it explores multi-agent coordination and adaptive behavior embedded in familiar domestic forms, named for the fungus whose networked growth visually overtakes the objects. The project won the Grand Prize at the Hardmode MIT AI Hackathon and was demonstrated at Anthropic Dev Day 2026.

images.caption: The Cordyceps objects—a radio, an articulated desk lamp, and a framed mirror—linked by white bulbous growths evoking a fungal network coordinating them as agents.
images.alt_text: Three black household objects against a white backdrop—a portable radio with extended antenna, an articulated desk lamp bent forward, and a framed mirror—each covered with clusters of white spherical growths that spread between them like fungal tendrils.

interactions.title: Three objects, one task, no hub app
interactions.description: Instead of a single humanoid assistant or a phone full of automations, a mirror, lamp, and radio split a request among themselves — each acting through its own form rather than a shared screen.

interactions.title: Behavior shaped by what each object already is
interactions.description: Each agent's response is constrained by its modality and physical affordances: the lamp answers in light, the radio in sound, the mirror in reflection — the design's premise that an object should act through the thing it already does.

interactions.title: Hackathon-floor demo of objects coming to life
interactions.description: Shown live as the Grand Prize project at the Hardmode MIT AI Hackathon and again at Anthropic Dev Day 2026, where the linked household objects coordinate in front of an audience.

interactions.title: Ask the room, not a device
interactions.description: Speak a request out loud and let the ecology decide who answers — the radio picks up the audio, confers with the others, and the most fitting object responds, drawing on the speech and audio inputs the system already takes.

interactions.title: The lamp leans toward what matters
interactions.description: Using the system's vision input, the articulated lamp could swing and brighten over the spot you're working at while the radio drops its volume — two agents negotiating attention without you touching either.

interactions.title: A morning handoff between objects
interactions.description: The radio reads the day's first headline aloud, the mirror's agent chimes in as you stand in front of it, and the lamp warms its color — a relay choreographed by the multi-agent coordination at the project's core.

interactions.title: Watching the network disagree
interactions.description: Because behavior is shaped by each object's relations to the others, a designer could stage moments where the lamp and radio reach different conclusions and resolve them out loud — making the coordination itself legible rather than hidden.

sources.title: Cordyceps
